question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a ContractCallRequest with gas=21000 (the minimum allowed by @Min(21_000)) so that scaleGas() returns Math.floorDiv(21000,10000)=2, consuming only 2 tokens from gasLimitBucket instead of a proportional amount, allowing the attacker to submit far more high-gas-equivalent transactions per second than the throttle intends, effectively bypassing the gas-per-second rate limit?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a ContractCallRequest with gas values just above multiples of GAS_SCALE_FACTOR (10000) — e.g., gas=21001 — so that scaleGas() floor-divides to 2 tokens consumed, while gas=29999 also consumes only 2 tokens, meaning the attacker can submit transactions with nearly 30000 gas while only consuming 2 gas-bucket tokens, undermining the gas throttle's accuracy and allowing disproportionate resource consumption?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: restore()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger restore(long gas) with a gas value that, after scaleGas(), adds tokens back to gasLimitBucket beyond what was originally consumed, if the caller passes the pre-execution gas limit rather than actual gas used, allowing the attacker to inflate the gasLimitBucket token count and bypass gas-per-second throttling for subsequent transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the sequential check order in throttle() — first rateLimitBucket.tryConsume(1), then gasLimitBucket.tryConsume(scaleGas(request.getGas())) — by submitting a burst of requests that consume all rateLimitBucket tokens but not gasLimitBucket tokens, causing legitimate high-gas transactions from other users to be blocked by the rate limit while the attacker's low-gas transactions pass through, effectively performing a denial-of-service against other users' transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a ContractCallRequest where the gas field is set to exactly GAS_SCALE_FACTOR (10000), but since @Min(21_000) enforces a minimum of 21000, can the attacker instead set gas=21000 so scaleGas returns 2, and then rapidly submit many such requests to exhaust the rateLimitBucket (500 req/s default) while consuming minimal gasLimitBucket tokens, starving legitimate users who need to submit larger gas transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: action()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a ContractCallRequest that matches a RequestProperties filter with ActionType.LOG, causing the full request object (including from address, to address, data payload, and value) to be written to application logs via log.info(\

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle()] Can an unprivileged external user bypass a RequestProperties filter with ActionType.REJECT by crafting a ContractCallRequest whose fields (FROM, TO, DATA, BLOCK, GAS, VALUE, ESTIMATE) do not match any configured RequestFilter expression, so that the filter's test() returns false and the REJECT action is never triggered, allowing the attacker to submit otherwise-blocked transaction types?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the probabilistic sampling in RequestProperties.test() — where RandomUtils.secure().randomLong(0L, 100L) >= rate causes non-THROTTLE actions to be skipped probabilistically — by repeatedly submitting a transaction that should be REJECTed, knowing that with rate < 100 there is a nonzero probability the REJECT action is skipped, eventually getting a malicious transaction through?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the counter.getAndIncrement() >= limit check in RequestProperties.test() — where limit defaults to Long.MAX_VALUE — by submitting enough requests to cause the AtomicLong counter to overflow from Long.MAX_VALUE to Long.MIN_VALUE, causing counter >= limit to evaluate to false (since Long.MIN_VALUE < Long.MAX_VALUE), thereby re-enabling a filter that was supposed to have expired after reaching its limit?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a ContractCallRequest with a DATA field containing a case-variant of a blocked function selector (e.g., using uppercase hex characters like '0xDEADBEEF' instead of '0xdeadbeef') to bypass a RequestFilter with FilterType.CONTAINS that uses Strings.CI::contains (case-insensitive), or conversely, does the case-insensitive matching cause unintended filter matches on legitimate transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a ContractCallRequest with a FROM address that partially matches a CONTAINS filter expression (e.g., the filter expression '04e2' matches any address containing that substring), allowing the attacker to craft a FROM address that deliberately triggers or avoids a REJECT filter by including or excluding the target substring?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that rateLimitBucket uses no SynchronizationStrategy (unlike gasLimitBucket which uses SYNCHRONIZED) to cause a race condition where multiple concurrent requests each see tryConsume(1) return true simultaneously, allowing more requests per second than the configured requestsPerSecond limit, effectively bypassing the rate limit under high concurrency?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttleOpcodeRequest()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that opcodeRateLimitBucket uses no SynchronizationStrategy to cause a race condition where multiple concurrent opcode requests each see tryConsume(1) return true simultaneously, bypassing the opcodeRequestsPerSecond limit (default 1) and submitting more opcode trace requests than allowed?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: restore()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger restore(long gas) with a very large gas value (e.g., Long.MAX_VALUE) causing scaleGas() to return Long.MAX_VALUE / 10000, and then gasLimitBucket.addTokens() to add an enormous number of tokens, effectively resetting the gas throttle and allowing unlimited gas consumption by subsequent transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a ContractCallRequest with the BLOCK field set to a specific BlockType value (e.g., LATEST vs EARLIEST) to deliberately match or avoid a RequestFilter with FilterField.BLOCK and FilterType.EQUALS, bypassing a REJECT filter that only applies to LATEST block requests by submitting with EARLIEST block type?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that RequestProperties.test() returns true when filters list is empty (return filters.isEmpty()), meaning a RequestProperties with no configured filters matches ALL requests, so if an attacker can somehow cause a REJECT-action RequestProperties to have an empty filter list, every transaction would be rejected as a denial-of-service?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a ContractCallRequest with estimate=true to bypass a RequestFilter with FilterField.ESTIMATE and FilterType.EQUALS set to 'false', allowing the attacker to submit estimate calls that avoid REJECT or THROTTLE filters intended to block non-estimate calls?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a ContractCallRequest with a TO address that is the zero address or an empty string to bypass a RequestFilter with FilterField.TO and FilterType.EQUALS targeting a specific contract address, allowing the attacker to submit transactions to unfiltered contracts while avoiding throttle rules?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a ContractCallRequest with value=0 to bypass a RequestFilter with FilterField.VALUE and FilterType.EQUALS targeting value='1', allowing the attacker to submit value-transfer transactions that avoid REJECT filters by setting value to a non-matching amount?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a ContractCallRequest with a GAS value that exactly matches a RequestFilter with FilterField.GAS and FilterType.EQUALS to deliberately trigger a THROTTLE or REJECT action, causing a denial-of-service for other users whose transactions happen to use the same gas value?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the for-loop in throttle() iterates over ALL matching RequestProperties filters and calls action() for each match, so that a single ContractCallRequest matching multiple filters with ActionType.LOG causes the full request to be logged multiple times, amplifying log volume and potentially causing log storage exhaustion?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: action()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a ContractCallRequest with an extremely large DATA field (e.g., megabytes of hex data) that matches a LOG filter, causing log.info(\

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a ContractCallRequest with a DATA field containing log injection characters (e.g., newlines, ANSI escape codes) that, when logged via log.info(\

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: throttle()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the check order in throttle() — rateLimitBucket checked before gasLimitBucket — by submitting a high volume of minimal-gas requests to exhaust the rateLimitBucket, then when the rate limit resets, submitting a single maximum-gas transaction that passes the rate check but is blocked by gasLimitBucket, creating a timing-based denial-of-service against high-gas legitimate transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/throttle/ThrottleManagerImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: restore()] Can an unprivileged external user cause restore(long gas) to be called with gas=0, which scaleGas() converts to 0 (since 0 <= GAS_SCALE_FACTOR), and the tokens > 0 check prevents adding tokens, but if the caller passes a negative gas value, does scaleGas() handle it correctly or could Math.floorDiv produce unexpected results that add tokens to gasLimitBucket?
